text:
A Melbourne radio host has proposed changing the national flag to feature a "quintessential symbol of Australia", citing confusion arising on social media during the Paris Olympics.

A Melbourne radio host has suggested a new national flag design based on a “quintessential symbol of Australian”.

3AW Mornings host Tom Elliott said he didn’t mind the Australian flag as it was, but there was a lot of “confusion” which had arisen on social media during coverage of the Paris Olympics about why the colours of the Australian flag differed from our national sporting colours.

“Why do we have a red, white and blue flag, and yet our national colours are green and gold,” Elliott asked.

The Melbourne radio host said it raised the question of whether the Australian flag should be changed before he proposed a design based on the symbol of a Kangaroo.

“The kangaroo is the quintessential symbol of Australia,” he said

“If you wanted to unite our sporting colours of green and gold, I reckon a flag that would make sense would be a green background with the yellow kangaroo in the centre."

The Melbourne radio host pointed out  the famous green and gold boxing kangaroo flag was flown during Australia’s historic victory in the America’s Cup.

“Now I'm not saying we have the boxing kangaroo as our official national symbol,” Elliott said.

“But a stylized kangaroo – you know, like the Qantas one, but without the wings – in gold on a green background. That could work.”

The 3AW host acknowledged Australia wasn't the only country whose sporting colours differ from their national flag – with Italy and New Zealand being two other examples.

Despite the Italian flag being green, white and red, Italian sporting teams play in blue uniforms, with the national football team is nicknamed the Azzurri – which literally translates to the Blues.

Similarly, New Zealand’s sporting colours are black with a touch of white, despite the country’s national flag being similar to Australia’s.

New Zealand held a three-stage referendum on whether to change its national flag in 2015 and 2016. The first stage involved voters selecting between five alternative designs, with the top two moving to a second vote.

The winning design was a black and blue design with a white fern on the left and a red southern cross on the right.

But in the final stage of the referendum, this new national flag was supported by just over 43 per cent of voters. This was compared to more than 56 per cent of New Zealanders who voted to retain the existing national flag.

In 2010 a national poll of 652 Australians aged 14 and above found  66 per cent supported retaining Australia’s existing national flag compared to 29 per cent who support changing the flag.
